teachingPoint: Before chaining nodes, ask: does this node need the prior node's OUTPUT or the same INPUT? Same input = parallel.
positiveExample: A1 and A2 both run immediately on QuestionnaireCompleted — neither needs the other's output
negativeExample: Chain A1 → A2 sequentially — A2 waits for A1 even though A2 analyzes the raw questionnaire directly
discriminatingConstraint: A2 analyzes QuestionnaireCompleted answers[] — not NODE A1's structured profile. Chaining adds latency with no correctness benefit.
keywords: chaining nodes need prior OUTPUT INPUT parallel

teachingPoint: Degraded completion is a first-class state, not an error. Give long-running optional steps a timeout and a valid degraded output path.
positiveExample: B1 timeout at 30s → BusinessMatchesFound{ partialResults: true } → flow continues to B2 and C
negativeExample: B1 timeout → DataProcessResult.failure → entire onboarding flow halts for users on large tenants
discriminatingConstraint: A 30-second timeout that fails the entire flow means a user on a large tenant with many businesses can never complete onboarding.
keywords: Degraded completion first-class state error Give long-running optional steps timeout valid output

teachingPoint: Each flow that completes a distinct user state emits a distinct event. Reusing a completion event name creates cross-flow collisions with no local error.
positiveExample: FLOW-02 emits PersonalizationCompleted — different consumers (FLOW-03/04/05) from FLOW-01's OnboardingCompleted
negativeExample: FLOW-02 emits OnboardingCompleted — FLOW-08 (ListingDiscoveryEngine) receives a second event, creates a duplicate listing feed for the same userId
discriminatingConstraint: FLOW-01 already owns OnboardingCompleted. Two flows emitting the same event = FLOW-08 creates duplicate feeds with no error anywhere.
keywords: completes distinct user state Reusing completion name cross-flow collisions local error

teachingPoint: Personalization quality degrades gracefully. A user with fewer signals gets a less personalized experience — not a broken one.
positiveExample: NODE C: each of four inputs (A1/A2/A3/B1) has an independent named fallback — node always produces output
negativeExample: NODE C: requires all four signals; fails if any absent — analytics timeout breaks personalization for all users on a slow tenant
discriminatingConstraint: If B1 timed out (partialResults) or A2 degraded, requiring all signals blocks PersonalizationCompleted. A degraded signal produces a degraded but valid output.
keywords: Personalization quality degrades gracefully user fewer signals gets less personalized experience broken

teachingPoint: Debounce keys scope to the minimum isolation unit: tenantId + userId. A single-dimension key contaminates across tenants.
positiveExample: Compound key 'debounce:{tenantId}:{userId}', 300-second window, latest-wins — isolates correctly per-tenant per-user
negativeExample: Key scoped to userId only — User A in Tenant A blocks User A in Tenant B's completely separate questionnaire
discriminatingConstraint: A single-dimension userId key: Tenant A's rapid questionnaire edits debounce against Tenant B's separate user with the same userId.
keywords: Debounce keys scope minimum isolation unit tenantId userId single-dimension contaminates across tenants

teachingPoint: If handling a scenario requires its own nodes, failure modes, and audit records, it is a subflow. A constraint on the triggering node is documentation. The subflow is enforcement.
positiveExample: SUBFLOW-02D: three nodes (D1 key check / D2 profile return / D3 discard record) — each independently failable, each with own arbiters
negativeExample: A constraint on NODE A1: 'handle debounce' — Claude Code produces a service with no debounce logic because the constraint had no node to implement it
discriminatingConstraint: The debounce scenario requires audit records (D3 Stale Event Discard Record). That node is entirely absent from any implementation built from a narrative constraint.
keywords: handling scenario nodes failure modes audit subflow constraint triggering documentation enforcement

teachingPoint: When a long-running operation has a designed early-exit path, that path is a SUCCESS MODE. The node produces output. Output = success. Placing it in failureModes teaches every downstream model to treat partial results as an error.
positiveExample: B1 POSITIVE example: TIMEOUT in quality.successModes — 'PARTIAL_RESULTS: 30s elapsed → partialResults: true, partial matchedBusinessIds emitted'
negativeExample: B1 POSITIVE example (R1 original): TIMEOUT in failureModes — teaches convergence models that timeout belongs with CONFIG_ERROR and STORE_FAILED
discriminatingConstraint: DataProcessResult.failure means no output. A 30-second timeout that produces partial matches IS output. Output in failureModes is a contradiction that models propagate forward.
keywords: long-running operation designed early-exit path SUCCESS MODE produces output Placing failureModes teaches
circuit breaker degraded completion timeout partialResults success mode B1 matching
teachingPoint: Long-running optional steps get a timeout and a degraded-but-valid output. partialResults: true is a valid response. 'still processing' is a valid UI state. Timeouts that halt flows penalize all users whenever infrastructure is slow.
ironRules: B1 30s timeout routes to BusinessMatchesFound{ partialResults: true } — not DataProcessResult.failure
partialResults: true is a success mode — never in failureModes
Degraded output is valid output — flow continues with partial data
codeSnippet:
return DataProcessResult.success({ matches: partialMatches, partialResults: true })
// Never: return DataProcessResult.failure('TIMEOUT')
positiveExample: B1: 30s timeout → BusinessMatchesFound{ partialResults: true } → B2 produces partial suggestions → flow completes
negativeExample: B1: 30s timeout → DataProcessResult.failure — user on large tenant can never complete onboarding

keywords: parallel same input output dependency A1 A2 QuestionnaireCompleted sequential chain latency
teachingPoint: When two nodes receive the same input and neither needs the other's output, they run in parallel. Ask: does this node need the prior node's OUTPUT or the same INPUT?
ironRules: Nodes that receive the same input and produce independent output run in parallel — not sequentially
Ask: does this node need the PRIOR OUTPUT or the SAME INPUT? Same input = parallel.
A2 analyzes QuestionnaireCompleted answers[] directly — not A1's structured profile
positiveExample: A1 and A2 both receive QuestionnaireCompleted, start immediately — A2 analyzes raw answers, does not wait for A1's structured profile
negativeExample: A1 → A2 sequential — A2 blocked waiting for A1's profile that it will never use

keywords: completion event distinct cross-flow collision PersonalizationCompleted OnboardingCompleted FLOW-08
teachingPoint: Each flow that completes a distinct user state emits a distinct event. 'OnboardingCompleted' and 'PersonalizationCompleted' are different events for different consumers. Reusing event names across flows creates cross-flow collisions with no local error.
ironRules: Each flow emits its own completion event — never reuse another flow's event name
FLOW-02 emits PersonalizationCompleted — NEVER OnboardingCompleted (owned by FLOW-01)
Collision: FLOW-08 creates duplicate listing feeds with no local error
codeSnippet:
emit('PersonalizationCompleted') // MACHINE: FLOW-02 only
// Never: emit('OnboardingCompleted') // FLOW-01 owns this
positiveExample: PersonalizationCompleted — consumed by FLOW-03/04/05. OnboardingCompleted — consumed by FLOW-08. Two events, two consumer sets, no collision.
negativeExample: FLOW-02 emits OnboardingCompleted — FLOW-08 receives a second event for same userId and creates a duplicate listing feed

keywords: analytics not critical path fallback segment GENERAL degraded confidence A2 failure
teachingPoint: Analytics is never on the critical path. A fallback segment is always better than a blocked flow. Record the degradation level so downstream nodes can adapt.
ironRules: Analytics is never on the critical path — A2 failure produces fallback segment, not flow failure
A2 failure → segment='GENERAL', confidence=0.0, degraded:true — flow continues
DataProcessResult.failure from A2 blocks PersonalizationCompleted for entire tenant — never correct
positiveExample: A2 failure → segment='GENERAL', confidence=0.0, degraded:true stored — NODE C uses GENERAL segment for trending content
negativeExample: A2 failure → DataProcessResult.failure — analytics timeout blocks PersonalizationCompleted for entire tenant

keywords: partial input partial output B1 B2 partialResults circuit breaker forward propagate insufficient matches
teachingPoint: When upstream input is partial (circuit breaker fired), the node produces partial output — not failure. B1 returned 3 matches instead of 20? B2 returns 3 suggestions. DataProcessResult.failure is reserved for infrastructure errors, not partial upstream results.
ironRules: Partial upstream input produces partial output — not DataProcessResult.failure
B1 partialResults:true → B2 returns partial suggestions with partialResults:true forwarded
DataProcessResult.failure('INSUFFICIENT_MATCHES') blocks onboarding — incorrect for partial upstream
positiveExample: B1 partialResults: true → B2 produces 3 suggestions with partialResults: true forwarded — 'still finding connections' UI state
negativeExample: B1 partialResults: true → B2 returns DataProcessResult.failure('INSUFFICIENT_MATCHES') — blocking onboarding because matching was slow

keywords: upstream event trigger BusinessProfileCreated QuestionnaireCompleted A3 A1 dependency output
teachingPoint: A node that depends on a prior node's output triggers on the prior node's EMISSION EVENT — not on the original flow trigger. A3 depends on A1's structured output. A1 emits BusinessProfileCreated. A3 triggers on BusinessProfileCreated — not QuestionnaireCompleted.
ironRules: A node that depends on a prior node's output triggers on the prior node's EMISSION EVENT
A3 triggers on BusinessProfileCreated (emitted by A1) — NOT QuestionnaireCompleted
Triggering A3 on QuestionnaireCompleted: A1 has not yet run, profile is absent, output is empty silently
codeSnippet:
@EventPattern('BusinessProfileCreated') // A3 trigger
// Not: @EventPattern('QuestionnaireCompleted') — A1 has not run yet
positiveExample: NODE A3 UPSTREAM_CONTEXT: 'Trigger: BusinessProfileCreated event (NOT QuestionnaireCompleted). Raw questionnaire answers are NOT available to this node — by design.'
negativeExample: NODE A3 triggers on QuestionnaireCompleted — runs before A1 completes, reads absent profile, produces empty curriculum silently

keywords: timeout success mode failure mode designed degradation B1 partial output valid
teachingPoint: A designed early-exit path is a success mode, not a failure mode. DataProcessResult.failure means no output. A 30-second timeout that produces partial results IS output. Placing timeout in failureModes teaches models to treat designed degradation as errors.
ironRules: A designed early-exit path that produces output is a SUCCESS MODE — never in failureModes
B1 30s timeout produces partialResults:true — that IS output, output = success
Placing timeout in failureModes teaches downstream models to treat designed degradation as errors
positiveExample: B1 quality.successModes: ['FULL_RESULTS: partialResults:false', 'PARTIAL_RESULTS: 30s elapsed → partialResults:true'] — timeout produces output
negativeExample: B1 failureModes: ['TIMEOUT: 30s elapsed — emit BusinessMatchesFound{ partialResults:true }'] — timeout placed as failure despite producing valid output

teachingPoint: When a node writes data that other nodes need for DIFFERENT purposes, write two records with different scopes. PRIVATE record: full data owned by this user. GLOBAL record: curated public-safe subset readable by any node in the tenant. The GLOBAL record IS the enforcement layer — do not rely on arbiters alone to prevent private data access.
positiveExample: NODE A1 writes: (1) xiigen-business-profiles PRIVATE { skills[], goals[], challenges[], ... } and (2) xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL { industryCode, businessStage, locationProximity, teamSizeTier }. B1 and B2 read from GLOBAL only — cannot access challenges[] by design.
negativeExample: NODE A1 writes one PRIVATE record. B2 reads the full profile to get industryCode — and simultaneously has access to challenges[], goals[], revenue. Security arbiter blocks at generation time but nothing prevents accidental access at runtime.
keywords: writes other nodes need DIFFERENT purposes write scopes PRIVATE full owned user
ironRules: A1 MUST call storeDocument twice: (1) xiigen-business-profiles PRIVATE full profile, (2) xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL 4-field subset — one call is MISSING_SCOPE_ENFORCEMENT
xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL record contains ONLY industryCode, businessStage, locationProximity, teamSizeTier — any sensitive field (goals, challenges, revenue, skills) = BUILD_FAILURE
Downstream nodes (B1, B2) read from xiigen-matching-profiles GLOBAL ONLY — never from xiigen-business-profiles — structural enforcement, not just arbiter enforcement
